question: Why do you want to study Mathematics at university?
lookingFor: Genuine, personal motivation rooted in intellectual experience — not a generic love of numbers or a career calculation.

bullets: Identify a specific moment or topic that lit the spark
Distinguish mathematics from related subjects you could have chosen instead
Show you understand that a degree goes well beyond A-level content
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The moment that really decided it was working through a proof of the infinitude of primes. The fact that you could settle an infinite question with a few lines of clean logic was almost shocking to me — I'd been good at maths for years without seeing it could do that. I tried physics and computing and enjoyed them, but I kept gravitating back to the structure underneath, and I realised what I actually love is that structure itself, not its applications. A maths degree felt like the honest choice.

commonMistakes: Saying 'I've always been good at maths' with nothing more
Naming a career like finance or data science as the real reason
Giving a motivation that would fit any STEM degree equally
followUps: What aspect of mathematics are you most looking forward to that you haven't studied yet?
Was there ever a point where you considered a different subject?

question: Why do you want to study Mathematics here specifically?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine research into the department — specific modules, research groups, or course structure, not generic prestige.

bullets: Name something specific: a module, a research area, a teaching approach
Connect it to a concrete interest or experience of your own
Show you understand the shape of the course, not just the brand
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent a lot of time looking at course structures and what drew me here was the flexibility to reach into logic and foundations in the third year — most courses treat that as optional extras, but it's the area I most want to explore properly. I also read a few papers from the number theory group that came up when I was going deeper on the Riemann hypothesis, and having that research culture nearby during an undergraduate degree matters to me. It felt like the course was built for someone who wants the breadth early and the depth later.

commonMistakes: Citing rankings or reputation with no substance behind it
Describing the department inaccurately — showing you didn't actually research it
Giving an answer that would work for any good mathematics department
followUps: What would you hope to get out of the department outside formal teaching?
Is there a module you're already a bit nervous about?

question: Tell us about a piece of mathematics you've explored outside your school syllabus.
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine independent curiosity — the ability to follow mathematical interest beyond what's required, and to talk about it clearly.

bullets: Name something specific and show real familiarity, not just name-dropping
Explain why it interested you and what you understood (not just what it is called)
Be honest about where the difficulty started — panels respect the limits of your understanding
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I got pulled into graph theory after reading a popular account of the Königsberg bridge problem — I liked that it started with something so concrete. I worked through an introductory text on combinatorics and graph colouring, and I could follow the four-colour theorem proof sketch, even if the full formal version is beyond me right now. What I found fascinating was how quickly you hit open problems: the colouring problems I started with led me to questions that nobody has fully answered yet, and that sense that the frontier is close is something I hadn't expected.

commonMistakes: Naming a famous result without being able to explain what it actually says
Claiming to have understood something at a depth that breaks down under one follow-up question
Sticking to topics that are really just A-level extension work
followUps: Can you explain the basic idea of what you read in a bit more detail?
What made you stop where you did — did you hit a wall?

question: What's your favourite area of mathematics so far, and why?
lookingFor: Genuine enthusiasm and the ability to articulate what specifically appeals — not just a topic name, but what draws the applicant to its character.

bullets: Name an area and go beyond 'I find it interesting'
Identify what it is about the style or approach that appeals to you
Connect it to something you've actually done, not just encountered
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Pure proof-based geometry is the area I keep coming back to. I love that you can start with just a few axioms and reach genuinely non-obvious results — the proof that the angles in a triangle sum to 180 degrees via parallel lines still feels elegant to me even though I've seen it dozens of times. What I like is the economy of it: you're not computing anything, you're reasoning, and the answer has to be true forever. I've been exploring projective geometry a bit on the side and the way parallel lines behave there made me rethink what I thought I knew.

commonMistakes: Naming statistics or probability just because it seems practical
Saying 'I like all of it equally' — shows no real self-knowledge
Picking a topic you can't say anything interesting about
followUps: Is there an area you find less appealing, and do you know why?
What's one result in that area that surprised you?

question: What do you think a mathematical proof actually does? Why isn't a very convincing example enough?
lookingFor: Understanding that examples establish plausibility but not certainty, and that proof establishes universal truth — with some grasp of why that distinction matters.

bullets: Distinguish between evidence and proof clearly
Give an example of a pattern that holds for many cases but breaks down (or might)
Show appreciation for what proof gives you that examples cannot
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A proof tells you something is true in every possible case, not just the ones you checked. An example — even a million examples — leaves open the possibility that case one-million-and-one breaks the pattern. A nice illustration of this is the statement that n² + n + 41 is prime: it holds for n = 0 all the way up to 39 and then fails at n = 40. If you'd tested the first thirty cases and stopped, you'd be confidently wrong. A proof closes that gap permanently, which is why mathematics can make claims that no other discipline quite can.

commonMistakes: Conflating 'very convincing' with 'proven'
Not being able to give a concrete example of a pattern that fails
Treating proof as just a formality on top of obvious intuition
followUps: Are there different types of proof you're aware of? Which do you find most satisfying?
Can you think of a case where your intuition about a mathematical statement turned out to be wrong?

question: What do you think is meant by saying a series 'converges'? Can you give an example of one that converges and one that doesn't?
lookingFor: A working conceptual grasp of convergence — that partial sums approach a finite limit — not a memorised definition, and real examples with some intuition attached.

bullets: Explain convergence in terms of partial sums approaching a limit, not just 'terms getting small'
Give a correct example of each — e.g. geometric series and harmonic series
Note the subtlety that small terms are necessary but not sufficient
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A series converges if the sum of the first n terms settles towards a fixed value as n grows — the partial sums have a limit. The classic convergent example is the geometric series 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + … which sums to 2. A divergent one is the harmonic series 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + … — the individual terms shrink to zero, which you might think is enough, but the sum still grows without bound, just very slowly. That surprised me when I first saw it — it shows that the terms going to zero is necessary for convergence but not sufficient, which is the kind of subtlety I find genuinely interesting.

commonMistakes: Saying a series converges if 'the terms go to zero' — missing the harmonic series counter-example
Not being able to produce a concrete example under pressure
Treating convergence as a purely algebraic manipulation rather than a limiting process
followUps: Does the harmonic series ever get past, say, 10? How long would that take?
What does absolute convergence mean, and why might it matter?

question: Do you see yourself as more drawn to pure or applied mathematics, and why?
lookingFor: Self-awareness and the ability to articulate what kind of mathematical thinking appeals — with some genuine reasoning, not just a guess at the 'right' answer.

bullets: Take a position and explain it rather than sitting on the fence
Show understanding of what pure and applied mathematics actually involve
Acknowledge that the boundary is blurry without using that as an escape from answering
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I lean towards pure, though I came to that conclusion slowly. When I work on a problem I notice I'm most satisfied when I understand why something is true, not just that it is — and I find myself less engaged when the point is to get a numerical answer for an external problem. That said, I think the divide is less clean than it sounds: a lot of beautiful pure mathematics eventually turns out to describe the physical world, and some of the deepest applied work is very abstract. But if I had to choose the end of the spectrum that keeps me up at night, it's the pure end.

commonMistakes: Saying 'both equally' to avoid commitment — shows no real self-reflection
Picking applied because it 'sounds more useful' without any deeper reason
Showing no knowledge of what either actually involves at degree level
followUps: Is there an area that sits at the boundary that appeals to you?
What do you think applied mathematicians actually do day-to-day?

question: Tell us about a time you got genuinely stuck on a maths problem. What did you do?
lookingFor: Evidence of mathematical resilience and productive strategies — trying different approaches, stepping back, seeking help appropriately — rather than giving up or waiting for the answer.

bullets: Describe a specific problem, not a vague 'hard topic'
Name the strategies you actually tried, not just 'I kept trying'
Be honest about what eventually unlocked it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was working through a problem on induction and hit a case where my inductive step broke down and I couldn't see why. I spent a while pushing at it the same way before I made myself stop and restate what I was actually trying to prove — writing it out in plain language rather than notation. That was what cracked it: I'd been assuming something that wasn't in the hypothesis. I've since made it a rule to restate the thing I'm trying to prove in words before starting, because half the time the confusion is in how I framed the problem, not the mathematics itself.

commonMistakes: Claiming never to have got stuck — unbelievable and a missed opportunity
Describing giving up or Googling the answer without reflection
Strategies so vague they don't show any real mathematical thinking
followUps: Is there a problem you're still not sure you fully understand?
What do you do differently now compared to when you first started working independently?

question: How would you prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers?
lookingFor: A clear, confident walk through Euclid's proof by contradiction — showing the applicant can reproduce and explain a classic argument, not just recall it exists.

bullets: Set up the contradiction: assume a finite list of all primes
Construct the number N = (product of all primes) + 1
Show N either is prime or has a prime factor not on the list — contradiction either way
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: You assume for contradiction that there are only finitely many primes and you can list them all: p₁, p₂, … pₖ. Then construct N by multiplying all of them together and adding 1. N is either prime itself, or it has a prime factor. But when you divide N by any prime on your list you always get remainder 1, so none of the listed primes divide N. That means N's prime factor must be a new prime not on the list, which contradicts the assumption that we had them all. It's one of my favourite proofs because it's so short and the construction feels almost like a trick.

commonMistakes: Saying N itself is always prime — it isn't, but it doesn't need to be
Getting confused about what N = p₁p₂…pₖ + 1 actually tells you
Knowing the proof exists but not being able to reconstruct it
followUps: Are there infinitely many twin primes? What do mathematicians believe?
Can you think of another result that uses a similar construction trick?

question: Can you prove that the square root of 2 is irrational?
lookingFor: A correct proof by contradiction, presented clearly — and ideally some appreciation of why irrationality is a genuinely surprising and interesting fact.

bullets: Assume √2 = p/q in lowest terms (p and q share no common factors)
Square both sides to get 2q² = p², so p² is even, so p is even
Write p = 2m, substitute, get q² = 2m², so q is also even — contradicting lowest terms
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: You suppose for contradiction that √2 is rational, so √2 = p/q where p and q are integers with no common factor. Squaring gives 2q² = p², which means p² is even, and since squares of odd numbers are odd, p must be even. Write p = 2m and substitute: 2q² = 4m², so q² = 2m², which means q is also even. But then p and q are both even, contradicting the assumption they share no common factor. The Greeks were apparently troubled by this when they discovered it — the idea that the diagonal of a unit square has no length expressible as a ratio is still a bit striking when you sit with it.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to specify that the fraction is in lowest terms at the outset
Making the jump from 'p² is even' to 'p is even' without justifying it
Presenting the algebra correctly but not understanding why it constitutes a contradiction
followUps: Is π irrational? How would you even begin to prove something like that?
Are there more irrational numbers or rational numbers?

subtopic: Cardinality and different sizes of infinity
question: Are there more real numbers than natural numbers? How could you even compare the sizes of infinite sets?
lookingFor: Awareness of Cantor's diagonal argument or at least the concept of bijection as the right tool for comparing infinite sets — and intellectual engagement with the strangeness of the result.

bullets: Introduce bijection (one-to-one correspondence) as the definition of 'same size' for infinite sets
Note that naturals, integers, and rationals all have the same cardinality via explicit bijections
Sketch Cantor's diagonal argument: any supposed list of reals in [0,1] misses at least one
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The right way to compare sizes is to ask whether you can pair elements one-to-one — a bijection. Surprisingly, the natural numbers and the integers have the same size that way: just interleave 0, 1, −1, 2, −2 … and you hit every integer once. Even the rationals can be matched to the naturals by zigzagging through a grid. But the reals can't. Cantor's diagonal argument shows why: suppose you had a complete list of all real numbers in [0,1]. You construct a new number by taking the nth decimal digit of the nth entry and changing it — so the new number differs from every entry on the list in at least one digit. The list was supposed to be complete, so that's a contradiction. There are genuinely more reals than naturals, which is one of the most mind-bending results I've come across.

commonMistakes: Treating all infinities as the same size
Knowing the conclusion without being able to sketch why it's true
Confusing countability with the size of specific sets (e.g., wrongly saying the rationals are uncountable)
followUps: Is there a size of infinity between the naturals and the reals?
Does the idea of different sizes of infinity change how you think about the number line?

question: In how many ways can you arrange the letters of the word MISSISSIPPI?
lookingFor: Systematic use of the formula for permutations with repeated elements — thinking aloud, handling the repeated letters carefully, arriving at the correct answer.

bullets: Count the total letters and identify how many times each letter repeats
Apply the formula: n! divided by the product of the factorials of each repeated count
Compute clearly: 11! / (4! × 4! × 2!) and simplify
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: There are 11 letters in total. Breaking them down: M appears once, I appears 4 times, S appears 4 times, P appears twice. If all the letters were distinct, there'd be 11! arrangements. But because repeated letters are identical, I divide out the over-counting: 11! divided by 4! for the I's, 4! for the S's, and 2! for the P's. That gives 39,916,800 divided by (24 × 24 × 2) = 1,152, which is 34,650. I find it's worth pausing to ask whether the answer is in the right ballpark — 34,650 distinct arrangements from 11 letters feels plausible.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to divide by the repeated-letter factorials at all
Mis-counting how many times each letter appears
Computing correctly but not checking whether the answer is reasonable
followUps: How would your answer change if the two P's had to stay together?
Can you think of a harder version of this problem?

question: What does a derivative actually mean? How would you explain it to someone who hadn't studied calculus?
lookingFor: Conceptual clarity — the derivative as an instantaneous rate of change or gradient — not just a rule for differentiating polynomials.

bullets: Explain the derivative as the instantaneous rate of change, not just 'the gradient'
Ground it with a concrete, intuitive example — speed as the derivative of position is classic
Mention the limit process underpinning it, even informally
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A derivative tells you how fast something is changing at a particular moment. If you have a curve on a graph, the derivative at a point is the gradient of the tangent line there — how steep the curve is right at that spot. A good way to see why it's interesting: if I know my position as a function of time, the derivative gives me my speed at any given instant, not just my average speed over a stretch. The key idea is a limit — you look at the slope of a chord between two nearby points and ask what happens as those points get arbitrarily close together.

commonMistakes: Jumping straight to differentiation rules with no conceptual grounding
Conflating average rate of change with instantaneous rate of change
Being unable to explain it without mathematical notation
followUps: What does the second derivative tell you?
Can every function be differentiated? What might go wrong?

question: What is integration, conceptually? And is it just the reverse of differentiation?
lookingFor: Understanding integration as area accumulation, as well as its connection to differentiation via the fundamental theorem — not just 'the opposite of differentiation'.

bullets: Describe integration as accumulation — area under a curve as a motivating idea
Acknowledge that indefinite integration is indeed related to antidifferentiation
Mention the fundamental theorem of calculus as the bridge between the two concepts
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Integration started for me as area under a curve — you slice the region into thin rectangles and add up their areas, taking the limit as the slices get thinner. That's a completely separate idea from differentiation at first: one is about steepness, the other about accumulation. What's remarkable is the fundamental theorem of calculus, which says these two ideas are connected: the area accumulated up to a point, as a function of that point, has a derivative equal to the original function. So in that sense integration is the reverse of differentiation, but the geometric insight about area is what actually makes it useful.

commonMistakes: Saying integration is 'just the opposite of differentiation' with no deeper picture
Not being aware that definite and indefinite integrals are slightly different things
No mention of why the two operations being inverses is actually surprising
followUps: Can every continuous function be integrated? Can every continuous function be differentiated?
What's an integral you can write down but not evaluate in closed form?

question: What is a matrix, geometrically? What is it actually doing when you multiply it by a vector?
lookingFor: Understanding of a matrix as encoding a linear transformation — rotation, scaling, shearing — rather than just a grid of numbers with multiplication rules.

bullets: Describe a matrix as a linear transformation of space
Ground it geometrically: what happens to the unit vectors tells you what the transformation does
Give at least one concrete example — a rotation matrix or a scaling matrix
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A matrix is really a linear transformation in disguise. When you multiply a matrix by a vector, you're applying that transformation to the vector — moving it to a new position in space. The columns of the matrix tell you where the standard basis vectors land after the transformation, and everything else follows from linearity. A simple example is a 2×2 rotation matrix for angle θ: it rotates every vector in the plane by θ without changing its length. Another is a diagonal matrix with entries 2 and 3: it stretches the x-direction by 2 and the y-direction by 3. Thinking about what a matrix does to space is much more illuminating than just learning the multiplication algorithm.

commonMistakes: Describing matrix multiplication as just a numerical procedure with no geometric meaning
Confusing the matrix with the transformation — they're different representations of the same thing
Not being able to describe what even a simple 2×2 matrix does geometrically
followUps: What does the determinant of a matrix tell you, geometrically?
What does it mean for a matrix to have no inverse?

question: You flip a fair coin and get heads ten times in a row. What is the probability of heads on the eleventh flip?
lookingFor: Understanding of independence — each flip is 50/50 regardless of history — plus awareness of the gambler's fallacy and the distinction between sequences and individual events.

bullets: State clearly that the probability is still 1/2 — the coin has no memory
Name the gambler's fallacy: the mistaken belief that past outcomes influence future independent events
Note the distinction between 'what's the probability of 11 heads in a row from the start' vs. 'given 10 have already occurred, what's the probability of the 11th'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It's still 1/2. A fair coin has no memory — each flip is independent of all previous ones. The gambler's fallacy is the intuition that 'tails must be due', but that's not how probability works for independent events. What's true is that the probability of getting 11 heads from the very beginning is (1/2)¹¹, which is tiny. But we're not asking that: we're asking conditional on 10 having already happened, and given independence, that conditioning changes nothing. The sequence of 10 heads is improbable but it happened — the next flip doesn't know that.

commonMistakes: Saying the probability is lower because 'heads can't keep coming up forever'
Confusing the probability of the entire sequence with the probability of the next event
Knowing the answer but not being able to explain why
followUps: If the coin were biased, how would you estimate the bias from the data you've seen?
What would it mean for the coin to be 'fair' in a formal sense?

question: Tell us about a mathematical conjecture or open problem that interests you — one that you don't need a PhD to understand the statement of.
lookingFor: Genuine curiosity about mathematics beyond the curriculum — the ability to state a real open problem, say why it's hard or interesting, and show independent reading.

bullets: State the problem accurately, not just its name
Explain why it's interesting or surprising, not just that it's famous
Show some awareness of the partial results or failed attempts, if any
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The Collatz conjecture is the one I find most weirdly compelling. Take any positive integer: if it's even, halve it; if it's odd, multiply by 3 and add 1. The conjecture says you always eventually reach 1. It's been verified up to enormous numbers by computer, and every specific case I've tried works — but no one has proved it holds for all integers, and mathematicians seem to agree it's genuinely hard, possibly requiring completely new tools. What I like is that a ten-year-old can understand the rule, and yet it has defeated serious attempts. It's a reminder that simple-sounding questions can sit right at the edge of what mathematics can currently do.

commonMistakes: Naming a problem but only vaguely gesturing at what it says
Choosing something so technical you can't actually explain it
Presenting it as if it's almost solved when it isn't
followUps: Do you think the Collatz conjecture is true? What's your intuition?
Are there open problems in the areas you hope to study?

question: Mathematicians often talk about a proof or result being 'beautiful' or 'elegant'. What do you think they mean by that?
lookingFor: Thoughtful reflection on what makes mathematics aesthetically satisfying — economy, surprise, inevitability — and ideally an example that illustrates it.

bullets: Go beyond 'short' — identify qualities like surprise, inevitability, or unexpectedly deep ideas
Give a concrete example of something you find elegant and explain why
Show this is a genuine aesthetic response, not a rehearsed answer
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think elegance in maths usually means that the proof reveals why something is true, not just that it is — and that you could not have reached the same conclusion more economically. There's often a moment of surprise followed by a feeling of inevitability: of course that's how it works. Euler's identity e^(iπ) + 1 = 0 is the famous example, and I do find it striking that five fundamental constants appear in one equation. But the result I personally find most elegant is the proof that there's no largest prime — it's three lines, it's completely elementary, and it settles an infinite question with a trick so simple it feels like it shouldn't work.

commonMistakes: Equating elegant with short — a one-line tautology isn't elegant
Citing Euler's identity without being able to say more than 'it has five constants'
Treating the question as purely subjective with nothing more to say
followUps: Is there a proof or result you find ugly or unsatisfying, and why?
Does the way a result is presented affect whether you find it elegant?

question: Why might the mean be a misleading summary of a dataset? When would you use the median instead?
lookingFor: Clear understanding of how outliers distort the mean, and a concrete context where median is more appropriate — ideally with an example.

bullets: Explain that the mean is sensitive to extreme values
Give a realistic example where this matters — income distribution is the classic
State when median is preferable: skewed distributions or when outliers are genuine data points, not errors
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The mean adds up all the values and divides — so one very large or very small value pulls it substantially. If you want to describe the 'typical' salary in a company where the CEO earns a hundred times the median worker, the mean salary will be much higher than what almost anyone actually earns. The median — the middle value — is unaffected by those extremes, so it gives a better picture of what a typical person experiences. Median is usually preferred for income, house prices, or anything with a skewed distribution where the outliers are real and meaningful rather than data errors to be cleaned.

commonMistakes: Knowing the definition of both without explaining when each is appropriate
Saying the median is always better — the mean has its own advantages (e.g. it uses all the data)
Not being able to give a concrete real-world example
followUps: When might neither the mean nor the median be the most useful summary?
What does variance tell you that the mean alone doesn't?

question: Can you give an example of mathematics being applied in a way you found surprising or impressive?
lookingFor: Awareness of how mathematical ideas translate into the real world — ideally from genuine reading or curiosity rather than a school example, and with some grasp of the underlying idea.

bullets: Name a specific application with enough detail to show real familiarity
Explain the mathematical idea involved, not just the headline result
Show genuine interest — why it impressed you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The one that surprised me most was learning that public-key cryptography — the thing that secures every online transaction — rests essentially on the fact that multiplying two large primes together is easy, but factoring the product back into those primes is computationally hard. I'd studied primes as pure number theory with no thought of application, and finding out they underpin all of internet security was genuinely startling. It's a perfect example of pure mathematics developed with no application in mind turning out to be the foundation of something enormously practical decades later.

commonMistakes: Naming an application (GPS, Google PageRank) without being able to say what the mathematics actually is
Picking a school-textbook example with no personal engagement
Overstatement — claiming to fully understand something that's actually quite involved
followUps: How secure is that cryptographic system now that quantum computing is developing?
Can you think of another case where pure mathematics found an unexpected application?

question: Have you encountered any abstract algebra — groups, rings, or fields? What is a group?
lookingFor: Any genuine engagement with abstract algebra beyond A-level — and if the applicant knows the concept, the ability to state the group axioms clearly and give an example.

bullets: Give the four group axioms: closure, associativity, identity, inverses
Provide at least one concrete example (integers under addition is cleanest)
Show some sense of why the abstraction is useful — different-looking structures sharing the same theory
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A group is a set with a binary operation that satisfies four axioms: closure (the operation keeps you in the set), associativity, existence of an identity element, and existence of inverses for every element. The integers under addition are the go-to example: adding two integers gives an integer, addition is associative, zero is the identity, and every integer has a negative. What I find compelling about the abstraction is that the same four axioms describe symmetries of a square, permutations of a set, and matrices under multiplication — completely different-looking structures, but once you prove something for groups in general it holds for all of them at once.

commonMistakes: Confusing a group with just any set with an operation
Forgetting one of the four axioms — inverses is the one most often missed
Knowing the definition but unable to verify it for a concrete example
followUps: Is the set of positive integers under multiplication a group? Why or why not?
What's a ring, and how does it differ from a group?

question: How would you explain what a prime number is to a bright ten-year-old who has never heard the term?
lookingFor: Clear, jargon-free explanation that is both accurate and accessible — showing the applicant can communicate mathematics rather than just perform it.

bullets: Give an accurate definition without using unnecessarily technical language
Use small, concrete examples rather than abstract description
Anticipate and pre-empt the obvious confusion (is 1 a prime?)
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd say: a prime number is a whole number bigger than 1 that you can't break into smaller pieces by multiplying. So 6 isn't prime because 2 times 3 makes 6 — it has a shortcut. But 7 is prime: there's no pair of smaller numbers that multiply to give 7, so it can't be broken down. I'd then list the first few — 2, 3, 5, 7, 11 — and let them test a few themselves. And I'd mention that 1 isn't counted as prime, which might seem odd, but mathematicians decided that including it would break a lot of useful patterns, so it gets its own special category.

commonMistakes: Using the word 'divisor' or 'factor' without explanation
A technically correct but completely inaccessible definition
Forgetting to address whether 1 is prime — the obvious follow-up from any ten-year-old
followUps: Is 2 the only even prime? Why?
How would you explain what 'proof' means to the same ten-year-old?

question: What do you think happens to the volume of a sphere as you increase the number of dimensions? Does your intuition help or mislead you here?
lookingFor: Engagement with counterintuitive mathematics — the volume of an n-ball peaks and then decreases to zero — showing the applicant can grapple with ideas that defy low-dimensional intuition.

bullets: Acknowledge that 2D and 3D intuition suggests volume grows with dimension
Note the surprising result: the volume of the unit n-sphere peaks around dimension 5 and then decreases toward zero
Reflect on what this means and why intuition breaks down in high dimensions
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My first instinct is that more dimensions means more space, so the volume should keep growing. But the actual result is the opposite: the volume of a unit sphere peaks somewhere around the fifth dimension and then decreases, approaching zero as dimension goes to infinity. The reason is roughly that in high dimensions, most of the unit ball's 'volume' concentrates in a thin shell near the surface — the interior becomes proportionally negligible. This is one of those places where geometric intuition built in three dimensions actively misleads you, which is part of why high-dimensional geometry is both hard and surprising. It's also why machine learning in high dimensions is so strange — the geometry doesn't behave the way you'd expect.

commonMistakes: Confidently asserting volume grows monotonically — showing only low-dimensional intuition
Knowing the result but having no sense of why it happens
Treating high-dimensional geometry as too exotic to engage with
followUps: What do you think the surface area of a high-dimensional sphere does?
Can you think of another place where high-dimensional behaviour contradicts low-dimensional intuition?

subtopic: Fermat's Last Theorem and famous problems
question: What do you know about Fermat's Last Theorem, and why do you think it captured people's imagination for over 350 years?
lookingFor: Accurate statement of the theorem, some sense of its history and the scale of Wiles's proof, and genuine reflection on why it is compelling — not just a recitation of facts.

bullets: State the theorem correctly: no positive integer solutions to aⁿ + bⁿ = cⁿ for n > 2
Note the contrast between the simple statement and the enormous difficulty of the proof
Reflect on what made the problem culturally captivating — the margin note, the centuries of attempts
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Fermat's Last Theorem says there are no positive integers a, b, c satisfying aⁿ + bⁿ = cⁿ for any integer n greater than 2. Fermat famously wrote in a margin that he had a proof but there wasn't space — a claim that turned out to be almost certainly wrong, since the proof Andrew Wiles completed in 1995 required 20th-century mathematics that didn't exist in Fermat's time and runs to hundreds of pages. I think it captured people's imagination because the statement is so simple that a school student can understand it, and yet it defeated the greatest mathematicians for centuries. It also turned out to connect number theory to elliptic curves and modular forms in ways nobody expected — the proof revealed a deep unity in mathematics that went far beyond the original problem.

commonMistakes: Misstating the theorem — confusing it with Pythagoras or with n = 2 having infinitely many solutions
Giving only the history without being able to say what the theorem actually claims
Treating Wiles's achievement as straightforward or just a matter of time
followUps: Are there still open problems in number theory that have a similarly simple statement?
Does the story of Fermat's Last Theorem change how you think about mathematical progress?

question: Have you taken part in any mathematics competitions or enrichment programmes? What did you get out of them?
lookingFor: Honest reflection on the experience — what the problems demanded that school didn't, what succeeding or struggling taught, and what it revealed about how they engage with mathematics.

bullets: Be specific about the competition or programme and what it involved
Go beyond results — describe what the problems were like and what they required
Reflect honestly on what it showed about yourself as a mathematician
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've done the UK Intermediate and Senior Mathematical Challenges and got through to the Kangaroo round in the Senior. What surprised me most was how different competition problems feel from school work — there's no template to reach for, and the first step is usually just spending time with the problem without any idea where it's going. I found I was much more impatient than I'd realised, and I had to practise tolerating that uncertainty. I also joined a local maths circle which covered topics like modular arithmetic and combinatorics not in the A-level syllabus — that's actually what first got me reading about graph theory independently. The competitions taught me more about how I think under pressure than about mathematics itself.

commonMistakes: Treating the question as purely about scores and medals with no reflection
Claiming participation in competitions to impress and then being unable to describe any of the problems
If no competitions taken: giving up rather than pivoting to enrichment, online resources, or self-directed reading
followUps: Is there a competition problem you remember that really stumped you?
What would you say to a student who's nervous about doing competitions?
